author: @NoahZark64
text:
In Star Fox 64, the difficulty of the game is completely determined by how well you perform right from the beginning. 

If you're a kid who's never played it before, there's no way you're gonna save Falco in time AND be smart enough to fly through all the arches successfully. But, if you do, the game says "Hey, you think you're hot stuff? Try out this level instead where there's hundreds of enemies." And if you're still a billy badass and kill 100 of them before the level is over, you get to take the big boy path. 

The whole experience is determined by your skill and if you fuck up, it sends you back to the easier path as if they're saying "Maybe you're not ready for this level yet". I love it!

author: @sdw-hv5ko
text: I really love Celeste's level of difficulty. Each level felt so hard when I did it the first time and took an hour or longer to complete, but you improve so much by completing it that when you go back to earlier levels they feel so much easier. And there's also so much endgame content that's there as an option. If you just wanna beat the main game and reach the top of the mountain, you can stop there and get a satisfying ending. If you wanna keep going they have B-sides and bonus levels that are hard as shit and unlock more content. I think it's an amazing balance of challenging players while allowing them to choose how much effort they want to put in, without any worries about not getting the "true" experience like Dunkey describes in this video.

author: @FulgrimTG
text:
Dark Souls has perhaps the best designed approach to difficulty ever, because the player can either make it incredibly easy, or incredibly difficult for themselves without ever breaking the confines of the game world. The level of challenge can be altered drastically - entirely by actions that you take in the game, as the player character. 

This is something I call "Organic difficulty" - as a stab at the old "artificial difficulty" meme that used to follow DS around. Organic Difficulty, as a design concept, can trace its origins as far back as RPGs in the 1980s, where the player could reduce the challenge of the game by simply leveling up their character. Instead of using an arbitrary menu, the player can alter the difficulty to their liking by utilising the game's mechanics. 

Skip back to the modern day, and I don't know of any game that has quite as many mechanics that alter difficulty as Dark Souls does. It's got leveling up, upgrading gear and flasks, enchanting, consumable items, summoning, equipment that is more effective against certain foes, exploitable environment (i.e. Taurus Demon plunging attack), sequence breaking, a variety of playstyles, messages with useful tips, etc, etc. 

The point is, the player can make Dark Souls incredibly easy, or incredibly challenging, entirely through utilisation of game mechanics, without ever opening the options menu.

author: @jacobzanatta1769
text:
I kind of feel like we're in a good spot in terms of difficulty these days. Take Uncharted. If you want a tough, relatively strategic challenge, we have Crushing difficulty. If you just wanna chill, blow away baddies and explore lost cities, we have Explorer difficulty. Having one does not devalue the other, especially considering that there are trophies specifically so people can prove they've beaten the game on harder difficulties.
Then again, we have games where the difficulty is a vital part of the overall experience (Dark Souls comes to mind), and games without much difficulty but are still fun to play (Like Just Cause 3).
It really just comes down to the fact that gaming shouldn't be exclusive to hardcore gamers, nor should it be completely catered for casual players. The great thing about multiple levels of difficulty is that we can provide for both at the same time.

text:
That last comment XDDDDD
Also, my thoughts exactly - that's why I love bullet hell, rhythm games, Trauma Center series, Picross 3D, Portal series, etc.
Because they're all based on rather simple concepts (dodge everything, hit everything, shoot two spacially connected circles, paint or break bricks, etc.), but get really crazy with just that one concept - i.e. your instinct kicks in and you do things without realizing how you're doing them.
Rhythm Heaven series is a great example - often times just "press one or two buttons according to timing/rhythm," but the stage explores those timings and rhythms extensively, ultimately bringing out your instinctive reactions. Idk how to explain it really, but those who have played it will understand what I mean.

author: @itzi7868
text:
This is a really good video that gave me some retrospective on difficulties and I think my take away from this is that when you design difficulties ideally what you should go for is something like this, 1 or 2 difficulties, or 3 if you're feeling spicy. I think this works best because besides the intended difficulty being true, this is because that's the difficulty you as the game designer know and understand best, but also if you're making a second difficulty that's an easier one or a harder one? You use that first intended normal difficulty as your base line for what you consider a more casual or hardcore play on your part.

This is how I think you can tailor multiple difficulties and how it should be done, because difficulties isn't just a numbers game and let God sort out the rest, you have to put intention in your difficulty to make it meaningful, even if experienced players won't understand the easier difficulties, because those aren't designed for them, those are designed for players who want a more casual play time while still having a good time playing and I think that works as broadly as you can while keeping up the quality of your game.
You won't be able to find the most scientifically accurate difficulty to skill level ratio and that's probably a fruitless endeavor to even take on, because games aren't completely balanced and the most fun ones aren't trying to be, they're trying to challenge the players but I think there is a sweet spot and like a good cook? You have to find it on your own and experiment with your own game design, keep experimenting and improving, that's how Nintendo got to where they're at and they've spent literal decades perfecting their formulas, proving to any aspiring developers that there's always room for more improvement and more experimenting within your own style.

For a 3rd difficulty that's something that can be more unique either to developers, or something like joke difficulties that could be fun. I'm thinking of something like Streets of Rage Mania and Doom's Nightmare difficulties, the latter is a joke one, while SoR's Mania is not exactly a joke but very close, rather it's something of an insane mode, something that's not expected to be beaten by most players and designed to be borderline unfair.

author: @Keladinus
text:
Difficulty is subjective, and enjoyment of specific difficulty levels is also subjective.

For example:
Back when Skyrim first came out, I initially tried to play it on adept, but I found it too challenging and after a little while, switched to normal.
This year, I went back to the game on a fresh new console with no saves, and with all my experience in it, playing on Adept felt natural to me.

Difficulty levels serve two purposes. First, to offer control over the game's intensity, much like hot sauce comes in mild, medium and hot varieties.  Second is to give both new and seasoned players a way to enjoy the game.  These are not the only reasons to include a difficulty setting, but they are the most common ones.

author: @normandygahn
text:
I disagree Dunk. Difficulty settings are fine and allow more people to enjoy a game than would otherwise. Easy is for people who really can't grasp the mechanics but still want to play, the normal or 'hard' setting is for the general audience, and the hardest difficulties are for those who've mastered the game and still want a challenge. 

The higher difficulties aren't intended to be fair-- in fact most games go out of their way to make them extremely unfair. Certain games feel punishing and impossible on the highest difficulties, to the point where as you said you'd need to exploit mechanics to win, and that's perfectly fine. They're essentially there as a bonus, for added replay value. 

I will say though that the best games usually provide the added challenge in a way that isn't so obvious. Extra collectibles that are even more difficult to obtain, secret bosses/levels, post-game content, etc. 

I won't begrudge games like Halo and other FPS's for opting for the difficulty selection route though. Hell even one of my favorite game of all time, Baldurs Gate, had difficulty settings which were extremely BS, just increasing enemy HP and the damage your characters took without even touching the wonky AI. Still gives you a reason to go back and try again, even with the same/similar character to see if you could do it. 

And I know the League stuff was tongue-in-cheek, but comparing it to single-player games is just plain dumb. I don't play it anymore either but I don't bemoan the time spent. Riot did a great job building their game and the community. They deserve their success.

And once all that is balanced in a sensible way then we know we will get a good game.

author: @boomboom6410
text: I feel difficulty settings are fine only if they are different enough from each other to have incentive to replay the other difficulties. For instance, if a game when set to hard and all that changes is the enemies health and damage then that's no fun or interesting but if you set it to hard and you get a hidden area, a few new enemies, one or two new bosses, or even just a slight change in a map layout can give more reason for a player to play hard mode. I want each difficulty to feel like you are playing an almost entirely new game while still staying true to the main game if that makes any sense.

author: @scottgrey3337
text:
As a strategy game player, I vibe with the part about difficulty levels. Few games truly make harder AIs more intelligent or capable, they just throw more numbers and resources at them, to the point that it’d be completely unsustainable if it was a player behind the wheel. Which in turn forces me to go with the most boring, generic strategy that always wins, because otherwise it’s just an unwinnable slogfest. 

Hardest difficulty shouldn’t be “we’re making the game broken, have fun surviving,” it should still feel like an intended experience.

author: @bluecube9515
text: Even though most games scale health and famage with difficulty, it is so bad when done poorly. Some games get it right, like DRG, where the scaling is deliberate and calculated, allowing you to typically kill most enemies in the same amount of time if you use weakpoints and good strategies to your advantage. But typically, it's a lot better to focus on the speed of enemies and the frequency of tougher enemies. That maintains a sense of consistency between game modes and encourages you to play smarter rather than more like an overoptomizing cowardly little leaf lover.

author: @danielstearns426
text: Halo 3: ODST is a prime example of a game that suffers from difficulty selection. You’re no longer an armored supersoldier, but a normal human whose mission has already failed before the game even begins. Lost and alone, you have to fight your way through hordes of genocide aliens to group up with your team and evacuate the city. The weight of the narrative is completely lost on lower difficulties as you mow down brutes and hunters like chaff. On legendary, your human weapons are nigh useless and you have to choose your encounters wisely because your next fight could be your last. I hated it on my first playthrough, but after playing on legendary it’s my favorite of the series.

Button mashing difficult: like dexterity of pressing the right buttons with perfect timing. (personally I don't enjoy games that are difficult in this way)

author: @calebdonaldson8770
text: A great example of difficulty handled correctly and difficulty handled incorrectly is the difference between the two SMB2's. SMB2 (Lost Levels) is a game so heart-wrenchingly difficult, it makes you question your existence as a gamer. This game is not fun. This game is frustrating to the bone, and drags out far too long with the same, derivative gameplay. Eventually, you not longer enjoy the game, and are just desperately trying to beat it at all costs. It is fine for a game to have a few super-expert levels, but when the entirety of your game is that and worse, you know you have f*cked up as a game designer. SMB2 (USA) is a game that handles difficulty with expert care. This game is quite challenging to say the least, but at the same time, boatloads of fun! Gameplay is varied, ever-changing, and always exciting. Levels are long, but there are fewer of them and each is unique. The game throws you curveballs here and there, but there is enough stability to keep your trust. It's difficulty comes from not only platforming, but also the required mastery of minigames and warp secrets, so your mindset never becomes stale. The key difference between these two games is that one is difficult and infuriating, while the other is difficult and thrilling. One of these games you will attempt once for a short period of time, and then never wish to return to. The other game pulls you into a great big hug and will never let you go and you will be fine with this. Which game do YOU want to play?

“Rule of Play”

Normal difficulty must be played or selected first before, “Real Play Ranking”is activated.  After your 1st death on normal mode, “Hard difficulty” is then used to determine your skill level for, “True Play”.  

1st death on hard difficulty counts as true defeat in game.  Continued play through deaths must be accrued, and tallied for total skill once game is completed on, “Hard mode”. Deathtracker determines your level of skill.

             Deathtracker

1 death.                    (Rank Master)
2-3 Deaths             (Rank Expert)
4-6 deaths.           Rank Professional)
7-10 deaths.          (Rank Skilled)
11-15 deaths.       Rank Amateur)
16-25 deaths.      Rank Average)
26-35 deaths.     Rank Beginner)
36-50 deaths.      (Rank Noob)

author: @TheScribeScribe
text: Baldur's Gate.  Fucking christ.  The difference between Insane and Legacy is ridiculous.  On insane enemies get double damage, use some stronger spells and some stronger enemy types show up more often.  On Legacy enemies get all that + increased saves, accuracy, dodge, health and probably some other shit I'm forgetting.  My berserker was struggling to kill the tiny fucking rats in the tutorial.  My first fast travel just under the Friendly Arm I got ambushed by 8 fucking bandits that skewered me with arrows in 3 seconds.  I realized I couldn't play my usual way anymore, I had to avoid fights at all costs.  So I made a sorcerer, ran down to High Hedge and got some invisibility scrolls bought with a diamond and ring of princes, some petrification protection + stone to flesh scrolls and abused the basilisk exp trick to max my exp and learn invisibility.  I then spent the rest of the game walking past every challenge.  Anything I had to kill I went slightly offscreen, spammed skull traps, (Dealing roughly 18 - 30 damage average depending on saves) invised, slept, repeated till dead.  The game wasn't fun anymore, the difficulty was in increased numbers, not ai.  It didn't force me to learn to strategize better, it forced me to out-cheap the game.  Bethesda games are the same way.  Every bandit has 1000+ hp and daedric armour when you're high enough level.  And that's on average, "Difficulty".

author: @BradenBest
text:
This is why I tend to play on the second hardest difficulty (unless I'm going for achievements or doing in-depth testing). The second hardest difficulty usually offers the best balance of fun and challenge. The _hardest_ difficulty is typically full of cheap shots and betrays the flaws in the game's design to the point where it ceases to be fun. Especially in retro throwback games where the difficulty only changes how many lives you have. Lives were created as a way to sell credits, and were never intended to be used for balancing a game's difficulty. It is not "challenging" to scrape through a bunch of bullshit in one attempt as opposed to six. That's just tedious and time-consuming, monotonous and grindy, frustrating and repetitive. Either put a checkpoint there or don't. Don't put a checkpoint there but then set the player back more after failing X amount of times. There is no good reason that two similar failures should result in two different setbacks.

Now if you excuse me, I must go play Aban Hawkins and the 1000 Spikes.

When Dunkey explained the difficulty spikes and how players just learn all the exploits and get the AI down to a tee... This is what ruined Skyrim for me. I can't go back to having fun since I know I could do better even on lower difficulties. Insane difficulties that just bloat enemy stats really bring out the worst in gaming. The game is no longer fun when you've basically learned the AI by heart and nothing is new or exciting anymore. Just drawn out fights.

author: @teneesh3376
text:
Right now, difficulty settings are extremely flawed. A lot of games changes the difficulty of the combat, but now everything else. Games like the 3rd tomb raider reboot, you can change the difficulty of the exploration, combat and puzzles individualy. But the also means the player is judging their own skill and guessing how good they are.

Titanfall 2 starts with a training arena. Depending how well you beat that, it recommends ehat difficulty to select 

My personal criterias if a game has different difficulty setting, the need:
1) A training arena to recommend what difficulty to select

2) Not being able to switch difficulty mid gameplay. Otherwise developers don't have to balance the game properly and players can cheese the game.

3) If it has multiple variations of gameplay, allow people to choose the difficulty for each variation

author: @DeadSpacedOut
text:
I know this is unrelated and i'm sure no one will ever see this comment but... when Dunkey explains that you want a game to have depth in a way in which you will keep getting better and better and that a cap keeping you from doing that is detrimental to the player, i not only agree, but say that this is the main reason that SBMM or whatever you wanna call it, which is in many modern games nowadays ruins the overall experience because you're always at that cap and can never get any better, there is no room for improvement, you're just forced to constantly go up against people just as good as yourself and quickly become complacent and give up on actually trying to be skillful in any way.

That shit ruins a Multiplayer game for me personally. Getting better and competition should always be staples of Multiplayer gaming.
